content:
# Leviticus Chapter 21 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70v–71r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_021_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_021_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 107–108 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 21 extends the holiness imperative of the Holiness Code (Lev 17–26) into the priestly body and household — a domain the preceding chapters did not address directly. Where chapters 18–20 governed the entire covenant community, chapter 21 turns inward to the priesthood, imposing on the kohanim a graduated scale of holiness obligations that exceeds those of ordinary Israelites. The chapter falls into three major sections: (1) restrictions on ordinary priests regarding corpse-contact and mourning (vv.1–6); (2) restrictions on ordinary priests regarding marriage (vv.7–9); (3) heightened restrictions on the high priest (ha-kohen ha-gadol / ha-kohen ha-gadol me-echav — "the great priest") regarding mourning, departure from the sanctuary, and marriage (vv.10–15); and (4) the law of priestly blemishes (mum) barring physically defective priests from approaching the altar or veil while permitting them to eat of the holy food (vv.16–24). The chapter's governing logic is the graduated holiness-map of the Sinai camp: the nearer to the divine presence, the stricter the demand for wholeness, separation, and purity. The ordinary priest bears obligations that exceed the layperson; the high priest bears obligations that exceed the ordinary priest; the chapter's conclusion (v.24) places the instruction in Moshe's mouth to Aharon, his sons, and all the sons of Yisra'el, signaling that priestly holiness is not merely a caste interest but a matter of public covenant significance. The key term throughout is the root q-d-sh (holiness/consecration) and its counterpart t-m-' (uncleanness/defilement), arranged in a relentless dialectic: the priest is the nezer — the consecrated crown — of divine presence in the community; therefore no defilement, however natural or humanly understandable (grief for the dead, attachment to a wife who is not a virgin), may touch him.]*

---

## Leviticus 21

**21:1** And the LORD said to Moshe: Speak to the priests [ha-kohanim — הַכֹּהֲנִים], the sons of Aharon, and say to them: For a dead person [le-nefesh — לְנֶפֶשׁ; lit. "for a soul/person"] among his people, no one shall make himself unclean [lo yittamma — לֹא יִטַּמָּא].

**21:2** But for his near kin [she'ero — שְׁאֵרוֹ; lit. "his flesh/near one"] who is near to him — for his mother and for his father and for his son and for his daughter and for his brother —

**21:3** and for his sister, the virgin [ha-betulah — הַבְּתוּלָה] who is near to him, who has not belonged to a man [asher lo haytah le-ish — אֲשֶׁר לֹא-הָיְתָה לְאִישׁ] — for her he may make himself unclean.

**21:4** He shall not make himself unclean [lo yittamma — לֹא יִטַּמָּא] as a husband [ba'al — בַּעַל] among his people, to profane himself [le-challelo — לְחַלְּלוֹ].

**21:5** They shall not make a baldness [qorchah — קָרְחָה] upon their head, and the corner of their beard [pe'at zekanam — פְּאַת זְקָנָם] they shall not shave, and in their flesh [u-vivesaram — וּבִבְשָׂרָם] they shall not cut a cut [lo yisretu saravet — לֹא יִשְׂרְטוּ שָׂרָטֶת].

**21:6** Holy [qedoshim — קְדֹשִׁים] they shall be to their God, and they shall not profane [lo yechalelu — לֹא יְחַלְּלוּ] the name of their God; for the fire-offerings of the LORD [ishei YHWH — אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה], the bread of their God [lechem Eloheihem — לֶחֶם אֱלֹהֵיהֶם], they present [hei maqrivim — הֵם מַקְרִיבִם]; and they shall be holy.

**21:7** A woman who is a whore [ishah zonah — אִשָּׁה זֹנָה] or one profaned [ve-chalalah — וַחֲלָלָה; a "desecrated/profaned woman"] they shall not take [lo yiqqachu — לֹא יִקָּחוּ]; and a woman divorced [gerushah — גְּרוּשָׁה] from her husband they shall not take; for he [the priest] is holy to his God [ki qadosh hu le-Elohav — כִּי קָדוֹשׁ הוּא לֵאלֹהָיו].

**21:8** And you shall sanctify him [ve-qiddashto — וְקִדַּשְׁתּוֹ], for the bread of your God he presents; holy shall he be to you, for holy am I [qadosh ani — קָדוֹשׁ אָנִי], the LORD who sanctifies you [meqadishkhem — מְקַדִּשְׁכֶם].

**21:9** And the daughter of any priest [bat-ish kohen — בַּת-אִישׁ כֹּהֵן], when she profanes herself [ki techal liznot — כִּי תֵחֵל לִזְנוֹת] by going whoring [liznot — לִזְנוֹת] — she profanes her father; in fire she shall be burned [ba-esh tisaref — בָּאֵשׁ תִּשָּׂרֵף].

**21:10** And the priest who is great [ha-kohen ha-gadol — הַכֹּהֵן הַגָּדוֹל] among his brothers [me-echav — מֵאֶחָיו], upon whose head [asher yutsaq al-rosho — אֲשֶׁר-יֻצַּק עַל-רֹאשׁוֹ] the anointing oil [shemen ha-mishchah — שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה] has been poured and who has been consecrated [ve-millea et-yado — וּמִלֵּא אֶת-יָדוֹ; lit. "whose hand has been filled"] to wear [livosh — לִלְבֹּשׁ] the garments — his head he shall not uncover [lo yifra — לֹא יִפְרָע] and his garments he shall not tear [ve-et-begadav lo yifrom — וְאֶת-בְּגָדָיו לֹא יִפְרֹם].

**21:11** And to any dead person [ve-al kol-nefshot met — וְעַל כָּל-נַפְשֹׁת מֵת] he shall not come; for his father and for his mother he shall not make himself unclean [lo yittamma — לֹא יִטַּמָּא].

**21:12** And from the sanctuary [min-ha-miqdash — מִן-הַמִּקְדָּשׁ] he shall not go out [lo yetse — לֹא יֵצֵא], and he shall not profane [ve-lo yechalel — וְלֹא יְחַלֵּל] the sanctuary of his God [et-miqdash Elohav — אֶת-מִקְדַּשׁ אֱלֹהָיו], for the crown of the anointing oil of his God [nezer shemen mishchat Elohav — נֵזֶר שֶׁמֶן מִשְׁחַת אֱלֹהָיו] is upon him; I am the LORD.

**21:13** And he — a woman in her virginity [be-vetuleha — בִּבְתוּלֶיהָ] he shall take as wife.

**21:14** A widow [almanah — אַלְמָנָה] and a divorced woman [u-gerushah — וּגְרוּשָׁה] and a profaned woman [ve-chalalah — וַחֲלָלָה] and a whore [ve-zonah — וְזֹנָה] — these he shall not take; but a virgin [ki im-betulah — כִּי אִם-בְּתוּלָה] from his own people [me-amav — מֵעַמָּיו] he shall take as wife.

**21:15** And he shall not profane his offspring [ve-lo yechalel zar'o — וְלֹא יְחַלֵּל זַרְעוֹ] among his people; for I am the LORD who sanctifies him [meqadesho — מְקַדְּשׁוֹ].

**21:16** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**21:17** Speak to Aharon, saying: Any man from your offspring [ish mi-zar'akha — אִישׁ מִזַּרְעֲךָ] throughout their generations [le-dorotam — לְדֹרֹתָם] in whom there is a blemish [asher yihyeh bo mum — אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה-בוֹ מוּם] shall not approach [lo yiqqrav — לֹא יִקְרַב] to present the bread of his God [le-haqriv lechem Elohav — לְהַקְרִיב לֶחֶם אֱלֹהָיו].

**21:18** For any man in whom there is a blemish — he shall not approach: a blind man [ish iver — אִישׁ עִוֵּר] or a lame man [o pisseach — אוֹ פִסֵּחַ] or one with a flat nose [o charum — אוֹ חָרֻם] or one with a limb too long [o sarua — אוֹ שָׂרוּעַ],

**21:19** or a man in whom there is a broken foot [shever regel — שֶׁבֶר רָגֶל] or a broken hand [o shever yad — אוֹ שֶׁבֶר יָד],

**21:20** or a hunchback [o gibben — אוֹ גִבֵּן] or a dwarf [odaq — אוֹ דַּק] or one with a defect in his eye [o tevelul be-eino — אוֹ תְּבַלֻּל בְּעֵינוֹ] or with a festering sore [o garav — אוֹ גָרָב] or with scabs [o yalefet — אוֹ יַלֶּפֶת] or with crushed testicles [o meroa ashekh — אוֹ מְרוֹחַ אָשֶׁךְ].

**21:21** Any man from the offspring of Aharon the priest in whom there is a blemish — he shall not draw near [lo yiggash — לֹא יִגַּשׁ] to present the fire-offerings of the LORD [ishei YHWH — אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה]; a blemish is in him — he shall not draw near to present the bread of his God.

**21:22** The bread of his God — from the most holy things [mi-qodesh ha-qodashim — מִקֹּדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים] and from the holy things [u-min-ha-qedashim — וּמִן-הַקֳּדָשִׁים] — he may eat.

**21:23** But to the veil [el-ha-parokhet — אֶל-הַפָּרֹכֶת] he shall not come, and to the altar [ve-el-ha-mizbeach — וְאֶל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ] he shall not draw near, for a blemish is in him; and he shall not profane [lo yechalel — לֹא יְחַלֵּל] my sanctuaries [et-miqdashay — אֶת-מִקְדָּשַׁי], for I am the LORD who sanctifies them [meqadsham — מְקַדְּשָׁם].

**21:24** And Moshe spoke [vay-daber Moshe — וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה] to Aharon and to his sons and to all the sons of Yisra'el.

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Overview

Leviticus 21 is the priestly holiness charter, applying to the kohanim the same logic of graduated holiness-demand that structures the entire Sinai complex. The chapter works by analogy: just as the sanctuary itself has zones of increasing sanctity (the outer court — the holy place — the holy of holies), so the priestly body and household must correspond to the degree of closeness to divine presence that the priest's office entails. An ordinary Israelite may mourn freely, marry a widow, and shave or cut his flesh in grief; an ordinary priest may do none of these (with limited exceptions for close blood-kin); the high priest may do none of them at all. The chapter's structural movement from ordinary priest (vv.1–9) to high priest (vv.10–15) to the blemished priest (vv.16–24) mirrors a three-tiered holiness typology: access to the altar requires not only right status (being of Aaronid descent) but also right condition (bodily wholeness) and right conduct (avoidance of defilement and profaning alliances).

The key theological term is *chilul* — the profaning (from challal, root ch-l-l) of the sanctuary, the divine name, and priestly lineage. To profane is to render common what has been set apart as holy; the priest's person, by virtue of his office, has been permanently consecrated (set apart) and cannot revert to ordinary status without a violation of divine order. The counterpart to challal is q-d-sh throughout: the LORD sanctifies the priests (v.8, 15, 23), the priests must be holy (vv.6, 7, 8), and their holiness is the reflex of the LORD's own holiness.

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v.1 — le-nefesh lo yittamma:** The formula is the chapter's governing phrase. Le-nefesh (lit. "for a soul/person") refers to the dead person (met — "dead" is not explicit here but is the understood referent; cf. Num 6:6 le-nefesh met, "for a dead soul"). Lo yittamma is the Nifal of t-m-' — "he shall not make himself unclean/defiled." The reflexive Nifal is significant: the defilement in view is not inflicted on the priest from without (it is not as if touching a corpse makes a man unclean against his will in a morally passive sense) but involves the priest's own action of approaching the corpse. The phrasing insists on the volitional character of the defilement. The opening address is to the kohanim as a collective — the priestly sons of Aharon — but the law is initially stated in the singular (lo yittamma — "he shall not make himself unclean"), focusing on individual priestly responsibility.

**v.2 — she'ero ha-qarov eilav:** "His near kin" — she'er (flesh, cf. 18:6; 20:19) denotes the closest blood-relatives: a list of six follows — mother, father, son, daughter, brother, and (v.3) the unmarried sister. These are the permitted exceptions for a kohen's corpse-contact. The list moves inward (parents → children → siblings), reflecting the degree of unavoidability of grief and the impossibility of denying mourning rites to one's own household. The priest is not inhuman; the law carves out these unavoidable exceptions precisely because to deny them entirely would be to demand not holiness but stoicism. The exception reinforces the rule by defining its limits.

**v.3 — ha-betulah asher lo haytah le-ish:** The unmarried sister qualifies for the exemption; the married sister does not. The reasoning is implicit but clear: the married sister has been "given to a man" — she belongs to another household, another priestly (or non-priestly) family; her death does not create the same domestic and family obligation that an unmarried sister's death would. The verb haytah le-ish ("has belonged to a man") is the standard biblical idiom for being married; the betulah ("virgin, unmarried woman") is defined here not by physical state but by legal marital status: she has not yet been given to a husband.

**v.4 — ba'al be-amav lo yittamma le-challelo:** This verse is among the most debated in the chapter. The literal rendering — "as a husband among his people he shall not make himself unclean to profane himself" — is grammatically difficult. The word ba'al (lord/husband/master) seems to indicate that the priest may not defile himself for a wife who belongs to another's household (i.e., a dead wife from outside the immediate family listed in vv.2–3). Alternatively, ba'al is understood as indicating the priestly ba'al — the priest's role of headship — which would be profaned by improper corpse-contact. Interpretations vary: some take it as "he shall not defile himself as a kinsman-by-marriage" (extending the exception to in-laws would be too broad); others as "he shall not defile himself in a capacity as husband for a woman among his people [who is not in the list]." The Targum Onqelos and later rabbinic tradition read it as a restriction on defilement for a wife; the LXX softens the ambiguity. EMET preserves the literal syntax without harmonizing.

**v.5 — qorchah / pe'at zekanam / saravet:** The three mourning practices forbidden to priests — head-baldness (qorchah, from qareach, "to make bald"), shaving the beard's corner (pe'at zekanam; pe'ah = "edge/corner," zaqan = "beard"), and cutting the flesh (saravet, an unusual noun from the root srt, "to cut/incise") — are the same practices forbidden to all Israelites in 19:27–28. The Holiness Code applies these universally in chapter 19; chapter 21 now applies them with additional force to the priests. The mourning rites in view are those practiced by surrounding Canaanite and ancient Near Eastern cultures: self-laceration, shaving, and baldness as expressions of grief were standard practice and apparently common enough among Israelites that the prohibitions are repeated several times. The theological rationale: such practices enact a ritual solidarity with the dead that the priest cannot perform, because his primary solidarity must be with the living LORD who consecrated him.

**v.6 — lechem Eloheihem / ishei YHWH:** Two terms for the priestly offering are juxtaposed: ishei YHWH (fire-offerings of the LORD) and lechem Eloheihem (bread of their God). The term lechem (bread) for sacrificial offerings appears frequently in Leviticus as a technical term for the food presented to the deity; the metaphor is deliberate — the sacrificial system is imagined as the provision of food for God's table, with the priests as those who serve it. The phrase ishei (fire-offerings, from esh = "fire") denotes those portions consumed by fire on the altar. The double designation — both fire and bread — encompasses the full range of sacrificial activity. The priest's holiness is required precisely because he is the one who handles what belongs to God.

**v.7 — chalalah:** The chalalah ("profaned woman," from challal) is a specific legal category alongside the zonah ("harlot/whore"): a woman who has been "desacralized," either through illicit sexual relations or through some other loss of the integrity that the law expects in a kohen's wife. The chalalah is the daughter of a priest who has herself been profaned (v.9 gives one way this happens: by going whoring). She is not merely "immoral" in a general sense; she is a woman who has lost the priestly family's consecrated status. The term gerushah (divorced woman) completes the trio of disqualified marriage partners for an ordinary priest. The theological rationale: a priest's household must be of a piece with his consecrated office; marriage to a woman whose marital history introduces categories of defilement or illegitimacy would compromise the integrity of priestly lineage.

**v.8 — ve-qiddashto:** The command is in the second person plural — addressed to the people (or to Moshe representing them): "you shall sanctify him." The people bear a responsibility for the holiness of the priests; it is not merely the priest's private affair. This is reinforced by the parallel in v.23 ("I am the LORD who sanctifies them"), where the divine role as ultimate sanctifier balances the human obligation to honor and uphold priestly holiness. The formula "for holy am I the LORD who sanctifies you" (ki qadosh ani YHWH meqadishkhem) is the Holiness Code's most characteristic self-identification: every holiness demand on Israel is grounded in a prior statement of divine character.

**v.9 — ki techal liznot / ba-esh tisaref:** The daughter of a kohen who profanes herself by going whoring (techal is literally "she has begun/profaned," from challal — a pun on the noun chalalah in v.7) brings disgrace not merely on herself but on her father: "she profanes her father." The penalty — death by fire — is the same as in 20:14 (the case of taking a woman and her mother). This is the severest form of execution and may reflect either a heightened gravity for priestly-family violations specifically, or the same logic as Judah's sentence of Tamar in Gen 38:24 (where a patriarch's household is implicated in sexual violation). The burning is not of a corpse; it is a form of capital execution. The juxtaposition with v.8 (the people must sanctify the priest) underscores the communal dimension: the priestly household's sexual integrity is not private — it is a matter of public covenant concern.

**v.10 — ha-kohen ha-gadol me-echav:** "The priest who is great among his brothers" — the high priest is designated by this circumlocution, not by a unique title. Ha-gadol (great) qualifies ha-kohen to indicate pre-eminence within the priestly order. The high priest's two marks of consecration are here identified: (1) the pouring of shemen ha-mishchah (anointing oil) upon his head — the ritual described in Exod 29:7 and Lev 8:12; and (2) millia et-yado (his hand has been filled) — the idiom for ordination/installation (lit. "the filling of the hand," referring to the placing of portions of the sacrifice into the ordinand's hands; cf. Exod 29:24). He also "wears the garments" — the eight vestments of the high priest described in Exod 28.

**v.10 — lo yifra / ve-et-begadav lo yifrom:** Two mourning gestures forbidden to the high priest but permitted (with restrictions) to ordinary priests: uncovering the head (yifra, from para' — to uncover/loosen; the hair was left wild and unbound in mourning: Lev 13:45; Ezek 24:17) and tearing the garments (yifrom, from param — to rend/tear; the standard gesture of grief: Gen 37:29, 34; 2 Sam 1:11). For ordinary priests, these are not explicitly forbidden (only the baldness, beard-shaving, and flesh-cutting of v.5); for the high priest, even these more restrained expressions of grief are prohibited. The high priest's person is so thoroughly consecrated that no mourning gesture whatsoever may disturb it.

**v.11 — ve-al kol-nafshot met lo yavo:** The high priest's prohibition is absolute: not even for father or mother may he approach a dead person. The ordinary priest may defile himself for his seven near-kin (vv.2–3); the high priest may defile himself for none. The phrase kol-nafshot met — "any dead souls/persons" — is emphatic with kol ("any/all"): there is no exception. The emotional severity of this demand — to be unable to be present at one's own parents' death, to not participate in the normal human rites of death — is acknowledged by the text's emphasis: "for his father and for his mother he shall not make himself unclean." The very parents to whom, under the Decalogue, he owes honor become the instances of the absolute prohibition. The high priest's consecration to the living God supersedes even filial piety toward the dead.

**v.12 — min-ha-miqdash lo yetse:** "From the sanctuary he shall not go out" — this restriction is not about permanent confinement but about the period of mourning or defilement: the high priest may not leave the sanctuary to accompany a funeral cortege or visit a house of mourning, because departure would constitute participation in the mourning process that he is forbidden to join. The sanctuary is both his domain and his protection: while he remains within it, the defilement of death cannot reach him. The phrase nezer shemen mishchat Elohav ("the crown of the anointing oil of his God") is theologically rich. Nezer (crown, consecrated diadem) appears in Exod 29:6 and 39:30 for the golden plate on the high priest's turban inscribed "Holy to the LORD." Here it is the oil itself — the shemen mishchah — that is called the nezer. The anointing oil is his crown; it marks him as consecrated beyond all ordinary consecration; to leave the sanctuary during mourning would be to defile that crown.

**v.13 — be-vetuleha yiqqach ishah:** "A woman in her virginity he shall take" — the high priest must marry a virgin (betulah), and specifically one who has not yet been married. The noun vetuleha (her virginity) in the masculine construction "in her virginity" is unusual; the point is that she must be in the state of virginity at the time of marriage. The rationale: the high priest's lineage must be beyond any shadow of questionable paternity or compromised consecration; only a woman who has had no prior marital relationship can provide this certainty.

**v.14 — almanah u-gerushah va-chalalah ve-zonah:** The high priest's disqualified marriage partners number four, against the ordinary priest's three (v.7, which lacks the almanah — "widow"). The widow is permitted to an ordinary priest (she is not on the list in v.7); she is forbidden to the high priest. The widow's disqualification for the high priest is the maximal expression of the same logic: the ordinary priest may not marry a divorcee or profaned woman or harlot; the high priest may marry none of these and additionally may not marry a widow. Only a betulah me-amav ("a virgin from his own people") qualifies. The phrase me-amav ("from his people") may specify an Israelite woman specifically, excluding proselytes or foreign women.

**v.15 — ve-lo yechalel zar'o be-amav:** "And he shall not profane his offspring among his people" — the concern is dynastic. The high priest's children bear a status derivative from their father's consecration; marriage to a disqualified woman would introduce profanation into the priestly lineage for generations. The word zar'o (his seed/offspring) links this verse to the blemish-law of vv.17–24, where zar'akha (your offspring) designates those who may be barred from priestly service. The priestly body (wholeness) and the priestly lineage (purity of descent) are parallel concerns: both affect the ability of subsequent generations to approach the altar.

**vv.17–24 — the mum list:** The second divine speech (beginning v.16) addresses a distinct issue: not defilement by corpse-contact or improper marriage, but physical blemish (mum — מוּם). The list of twelve conditions (vv.18–20) covers a range of physical states that would disqualify a priest from approaching the altar or the veil: blindness (iver), lameness (pisseach), flat nose (charum — possibly "slit/deformed nose"), elongated limb (sarua — "extended/distorted"), broken foot (shever regel), broken hand (shever yad), hunchback (gibben), dwarfism or thinness (daq — "thin/small"), eye-defect (tevelul be-eino — a clouding or spot in the eye), festering sore (garav — a type of skin lesion), scabs (yalefet — another skin condition), and crushed testicles (meroa ashekh — lit. "crushed of testicle"). The list is notable for including both congenital conditions (blindness, lameness, short stature) and acquired or acute ones (broken foot, skin disease), suggesting that the disqualification is based on current physical state, not hereditary condition alone.

**v.22 — mi-qodesh ha-qodashim u-min-ha-qedashim yokhel:** The blemished priest is explicitly permitted to eat of the priestly food — both the most holy things (qodesh ha-qodashim: the portions eaten in the sanctuary precincts, cf. 6:17–18; 7:6) and the holy things (qedashim: portions that may be eaten by the priestly household, cf. 10:14; 22:10–14). He loses only the right to officiate at the altar and to enter the inner sanctuary. His priestly identity and priestly portion are intact; only his cultic function is restricted. This is a legally and theologically important distinction: physical blemish disqualifies from liturgical action but not from priestly status or priestly sustenance.

**v.23 — el-ha-parokhet lo yavo ve-el-ha-mizbeach lo yiggash:** The two foci of prohibited access are the parokhet (veil/curtain — the inner veil separating the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies; Exod 26:31–35) and the mizbeach (altar — the altar of burnt offering in the outer court). The blemished priest may not approach either the most sacred interior space nor the sacrificial focal point of the outer court. The verb yiggash (from nagash — to draw near, to approach in a formal sense) appears in cultic contexts for the priestly approach to the altar (cf. 9:7, 8; Exod 30:20). The formula "for a blemish is in him" (ki mum bo) is repeated from vv.17 and 21 as the reiterated justification; it is not a verdict but a physical-status description that carries automatic liturgical consequence.

**v.23 — et-miqdashay meqadsham:** The plural miqdashay ("my sanctuaries") is unusual; in the singular it appears throughout the chapter (vv.12: miqdash Elohav). The plural may encompass both the altar and the veil as two distinct sacred focal points mentioned in this verse, or it may be a broader reference to the sacred spaces as a whole. "I am the LORD who sanctifies them" — meqadsham, with the suffix referring to the sanctuaries — closes the section with the same formula that closed vv.8 and 15 (meqadishkhem, meqadesho), reinforcing the chapter's governing theology: the LORD is the ultimate source and ground of all sanctification, whether of priests, of people, or of sacred spaces.

**v.24 — vay-daber Moshe el-Aharon ve-el-banav ve-el kol benei Yisra'el:** The chapter closes with a transmission formula rather than a content summary. Moshe speaks to three groups: Aharon (the high priest), his sons (the ordinary priests), and all the sons of Yisra'el (the whole covenant people). The triadic address ensures that the priestly holiness laws are public knowledge — not esoteric regulations known only to the priesthood, but shared understanding governing the community's relationship to the altar and the sanctuary. The people must know these laws in order to honor and support priestly holiness (v.8: "you shall sanctify him"), to know who may approach the altar and who may not, and to understand why a blemished priest may eat but not serve. The instruction is complete and public.

---

## Key Term Table

| Hebrew | Transliteration | EMET Rendering | Notes |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| לֹא יִטַּמָּא | lo yittamma | "he shall not make himself unclean" | Nifal reflexive of t-m-'; volitional defilement |
| לְנֶפֶשׁ | le-nefesh | "for a dead person" (lit. "for a soul") | Nefesh = person; here contextually the dead person |
| שְׁאֵרוֹ | she'ero | "his near kin" (lit. "his flesh") | Root sh-'-r; close blood-relation by consanguinity |
| הַבְּתוּלָה | ha-betulah | "the virgin" | Unmarried woman; legal rather than solely physical state |
| קָרְחָה | qorchah | "baldness" | Mourning-baldness; root q-r-ch |
| פְּאַת זְקָנָם | pe'at zekanam | "the corner of their beard" | Pe'ah = edge/corner; zaqan = beard |
| שָׂרָטֶת | saravet | "a cut" | Rare noun; flesh-incision as mourning rite |
| לְחַלְּלוֹ | le-challelo | "to profane himself" | Root ch-l-l; profaning = rendering common what is holy |
| אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה | ishei YHWH | "the fire-offerings of the LORD" | From esh (fire); technical term for altar-consumed portions |
| לֶחֶם אֱלֹהֵיהֶם | lechem Eloheihem | "the bread of their God" | Sacrificial food as provision for the divine table |
| זֹנָה | zonah | "a whore" | Woman of illicit sexual conduct; cf. 19:29 |
| חֲלָלָה | chalalah | "a profaned woman" | Woman who has lost consecrated priestly-family status |
| גְּרוּשָׁה | gerushah | "a divorced woman" | Legal status; lit. "one driven away" |
| הַכֹּהֵן הַגָּדוֹל | ha-kohen ha-gadol | "the great priest" / high priest | Lit. "the priest, the great one"; standard title |
| נֵזֶר שֶׁמֶן מִשְׁחַת אֱלֹהָיו | nezer shemen mishchat Elohav | "the crown of the anointing oil of his God" | Nezer = consecrated diadem/crown; mishchah = anointing |
| מִלֵּא אֶת-יָדוֹ | mille et-yado | "his hand has been filled" | Idiom for priestly ordination/installation |
| אַלְמָנָה | almanah | "a widow" | Disqualified for high priest (not for ordinary priest) |
| מוּם | mum | "blemish / defect" | Physical condition disqualifying from altar-approach |
| עִוֵּר | iver | "blind" | First in the blemish list |
| פִסֵּחַ | pisseach | "lame" | Lame/limping; root p-s-ch |
| חָרֻם | charum | "flat-nosed / disfigured of nose" | Uncertain exact sense; prob. abnormal nose |
| שָׂרוּעַ | sarua | "one with a limb too long" | Root sr-': extended/distorted beyond normal |
| גִבֵּן | gibben | "hunchback" | Spinal or postural deformity |
| דַּק | daq | "dwarf" (lit. "thin/small") | May denote emaciated or of unusually small stature |
| תְּבַלֻּל | tevelul | "a defect in his eye" | Spot/clouding; cataract or corneal opacity |
| גָרָב | garav | "festering sore" | Skin disease; exact diagnosis uncertain |
| יַלֶּפֶת | yalefet | "scabs" | Another skin condition; LXX renders as lichenos |
| מְרוֹחַ אָשֶׁךְ | meroa ashekh | "crushed testicles" | Lit. "bruised/crushed of testicle"; genital injury |
| קֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים | qodesh ha-qodashim | "most holy things" | The holiest grade of sacrificial portions |
| הַפָּרֹכֶת | ha-parokhet | "the veil" | Inner curtain before the Holy of Holies; Exod 26:31 |

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 107–108)

**v.1 — le-nefesh:** LXX *epi psyche tethnekuia* — "for a soul that has died" — adds the participle *tethnekuia* ("dead") explicitly, where the MT's le-nefesh is understood from context. The LXX removes the grammatical ellipsis of the MT and makes the referent explicit, a characteristic tendency of the Greek toward syntactic clarity.

**v.3 — ha-betulah asher lo haytah le-ish:** LXX *ten parthenon ten engys autes ten me ekdedomenon andri* — "the virgin near to him who has not been given to a husband" — a precise and extended rendering that captures both the legal marital status (me ekdedomenon — "not given out [in marriage]") and the proximity (ten engys autes — "the one near to him"). The Greek *parthenos* (virgin) corresponds exactly to the Hebrew betulah.

**v.4 — ba'al be-amav lo yittamma le-challelo:** LXX *ou mianthesetai exaiphneses en to lao autou tou bebelosai eauton* — "he shall not be defiled suddenly among his people so as to profane himself." The LXX reads the difficult ba'al differently from the MT; the Greek exaiphneses ("suddenly/at once") may reflect a reading of ba'al as ba-po'al ("in the action/suddenly") or may represent an interpretive paraphrase resolving the ambiguity. The LXX consistently avoids leaving the ba'al/husband difficulty unexplained.

**v.5 — qorchah:** LXX *phalakoson ou phalakothesetai he kephale autou* — "they shall not make a baldness on their head." The Greek *phalakoson* (baldness) corresponds to qorchah; the LXX renders the prohibition plainly without the infinitive-absolute intensification of the Hebrew.

**v.7 — chalalah:** LXX *bebelomenen* — "one who is desecrated/profaned." The Greek *bebelos* (profane, common, desecrated) is the standard LXX equivalent for challal; it captures the technical legal sense of removed sanctification while losing the specific Hebrew root-play between chalalah (v.7) and challel (to profane, throughout the chapter).

**v.10 — ha-kohen ha-gadol me-echav:** LXX *ho hiereus ho megas apo ton adelphon autou* — "the great priest from among his brothers" — a precise word-for-word rendering. The LXX does not use a special compound title for the high priest (as English "High Priest" does) but preserves the Hebrew's own circumlocution.

**v.10 — nezer shemen mishchat Elohav:** LXX *o epikechytai ep' auton to elaion tes chriseos* — "upon whom the oil of anointing has been poured" — the LXX paraphrases rather than rendering nezer literally; it omits "crown" (nezer) and translates only the anointing oil. This is a significant departure: the Hebrew nezer establishes the anointing oil as a *diadem* — a royal-sacral investiture — while the LXX reduces it to a description of the ritual act. The theological resonance of nezer (crown, Nazirite dedication, consecration) is lost.

**v.11 — ve-al kol-nafshot met lo yavo:** LXX *epi pase psyche tethnekyia ou eiseleusetai olos* — "upon any dead soul he shall not enter at all" — adding *olos* ("at all, in any way") to make the absolute prohibition emphatic. The LXX correctly captures the kol ("any/all") of the MT with the added force of the Greek particle.

**v.12 — nezer:** LXX renders this entire clause *hoti to hagiasma to elaion tes chriseos to kyriou ep' autou estin* — "for the holy thing, the oil of the anointing of the LORD, is upon him." Again the LXX avoids "crown" (nezer) and frames the anointing oil as a hagiasma ("holy thing"). The Vaticanus reading is notably full here and may reflect an attempt to explain the nezer concept through expansion.

**v.14 — be-vetuleha yiqqach ishah:** LXX *he de en to genei autou leepsetai gynaika* — "but from his own kin he shall take a woman" — the LXX renders be-vetuleha ("in her virginity") as en to genei autou ("in his kin"), apparently understanding the phrase as specifying lineage rather than virginal status. This represents a genuine divergence from the MT: the LXX high-priest marriage law appears to emphasize endogamy (marriage within priestly kin) rather than virginity per se. The MT's explicit betulah requirement is lost in the Greek at this point.

**v.17 — mum:** LXX *momos* — the Greek *momos* (blemish, defect, fault) is a direct loanword-correspondence to the Hebrew mum. In Homer, momos is the god of blame and ridicule; in the LXX it becomes the standard rendering of the priestly blemish concept.

**v.18 — charum:** LXX *kolobon rhin* — "shortened/truncated nose" — taking charum as a nasal deformity involving shortness rather than flatness. The LXX interpretation (kolob- = docked/shortened) is one of several ancient renderings; the exact nature of the condition remains uncertain. Vaticanus preserves kolobon rhin consistently.

**v.20 — tevelul be-eino:** LXX *ephaeloutos* — probably "with a white spot/cloudiness in the eye," corresponding to the cataracts or leucoma understanding of tevelul. The Greek medical-descriptive term attempts a diagnosis where the Hebrew term is obscure.

**v.20 — meroa ashekh:** LXX *monorchis* — "one-testicled" (lit. "having only one testicle"). The LXX interprets the Hebrew meroa ashekh ("crushed/bruised testicle") as monorchism (having only one testis) rather than as trauma to existing organs. This is a significant reinterpretation that moves from an injury-based reading to a congenital-condition reading. The MT's meroa (from ruach/roa — to crush/break) clearly denotes traumatic injury rather than congenital absence.

**v.22 — mi-qodesh ha-qodashim u-min-ha-qedashim:** LXX *apo ton hagion ton hagion kai apo ton hagion phagetal* — "from the holy things of holy things and from the holy things he shall eat." The LXX mirrors the MT's distinction between the two grades of holiness precisely. The right of the blemished priest to eat is affirmed in the Greek as clearly as in the Hebrew.

**v.23 — ha-parokhet:** LXX *to katapetasma* — the standard LXX term for the Temple veil; *katapetasma* (from *katapetannymi* — to spread down/over) corresponds exactly to the Hebrew parokhet as the great inner curtain. The same word is used in the NT for the veil torn at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45).

---

*EMET translation completed. Leviticus 21:1–24. All 24 verses rendered. Source: Leningrad Codex folios 70v–71r. LXX: Vaticanus pp. 107–108.*
